diagrams.title: DFS Timestamp Number Line
diagrams.description: Visual representation of discovery (d) and finish (f) times as intervals on a number line, showing nested structure for ancestor-descendant relationships
elements: Vertex B: interval [2,3] nested in A
Vertex C: interval [4,5] nested in A
Vertex D: interval [6,7] nested in A
Vertex E: interval [8,9] nested in A
Annotations showing: nested = ancestor, disjoint = no relation
diagrams.purpose: Illustrate parenthesis theorem: nested intervals indicate ancestor relationship; helps visualize edge classification and DFS tree structure

diagrams.title: Edge Classification in DFS
diagrams.description: Diagram showing four edge types in a DFS tree with directed edges labeled
elements: Tree edge: solid downward arrow to new node
Back edge: curved arrow upward to ancestor
Forward edge: downward arrow to already-visited descendant
Cross edge: horizontal arrow to different subtree or finished vertex
Color coding: GRAY = in-progress, BLACK = finished
diagrams.purpose: Visual aid to distinguish tree, back, forward, and cross edges; essential for cycle detection and graph analysis problems

diagrams.title: DFS vs BFS Traversal Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of DFS and BFS on same graph showing different exploration orders
elements: Same graph: root with 3 branches, varying depths
DFS path: deep into one branch before others, shows recursion stack building
diagrams.purpose: Highlight fundamental difference: DFS goes deep (stack), BFS goes wide (queue); helps students understand when to apply each
